metadata.title: VectorMapNet: End-to-end Vectorized HD Map Learning

sections.title: Motivations
content: Autonomous driving systems require an understanding of map elements on the road, including lanes, pedestrian crossing, and traffic signs, to navigate around the world.
In contrast, we focus on using onboard sensors, including LiDARs and cameras, to estimate map elements on-the-fly.

figures.description: Traditional HD map annotation pipeline showing manual labor and complexity
VectorMapNet end-to-end pipeline overview from sensor data to vectorized map

sections.title: Contributions
content: VectorMapNet is an end-to-end mapping approach.
We utilizepolylineto accommodate the heterogeneous nature of map elements.
Our method achieves promising performance in online semantic HD map learning tasks.

sections.title: Problem Formulation
content: Similar to HDMapNet, our task is to generate vectorized map elements using data from onboard sensors of autonomous vehicle, such as RGB cameras and/or LiDARs. These map elements include:
Lane dividers: boundaries dividing lanes on the road, usually straight lines.
Road boundaries: boundaries of roads separating roads and sidewalks, typically irregularly-shaped curves of arbitrary lengths.
Pedestrian crossings: regions with white markings indicating legal pedestrian crossing points, typically represented as polygons.
Centerlines: line segments that commonly used for driving direction, vehicle positioning, and navigation.

sections.title: Why use polyline to represent maps?
content: Using polylines to represent map elements has three main advantages:
Polylines are aflexible primitivethat can represent complex geometric elements in HD maps.
The order of polyline vertices is a natural way to encodethe direction of map elements.
The polyline representation has beenwidely used by downstream tasks, such as motion forecasting.

sections.title: VectorMapNet
content: VectorMapNet, an end-to-end model, is designed to represent amapwith a sparse set ofpolylinesthus formulating the task as a sparse set detection problem. In our approach, we divide the task into three distinct components:
(1) ABEV Feature Extractor (A variant of IPM)that lifts various sensor modality inputs into a canonical feature space (BEV space).
(2) Amap element detector (DETR-like Transformer decoder)that locates and classifies all map elements by predicting element keypoints Aiand their class labels li.
(3) APolyline Generator (Vanilla Transformer decoder)that produces a sequence of ordered polyline vertices which describes the local geometry of each detected map element Ai, li.

figures.description: Detailed architecture of VectorMapNet including BEV Feature Extractor, Map Element Detector, and Polyline Generator
Comparison of different keypoint representation methods: Bounding Box, SME, and Extreme Points

sections.title: VectorMapNet for Motion Forecasting
content: To evaluate the capacity of our method and to investigate its usefulness in downstream tasks, we put our predicted HD map to the test within a motion forecasting task.

tables.description: Motion forecasting performance table comparing Trajectory only, Trajectory with Ground Truth Map, and Trajectory with Predicted Map

sections.title: Quantitative Results
content: We report the average precision that uses Chamfer distance and Fréchet Distance as the threshold.

tables.description: Quantitative results on nuScenes dataset comparing various methods like STSU, HDMapNet, and VectorMapNet variants
Comparison of keypoint representations using Fréchet and Chamfer distances

sections.title: Qualitative Results
content: Using polylines as primitives has brought us two benefits compared with baselines:
Polylines effectively encode the detailed map geometries, e.g., the corners of boundaries (red ellipses).

Polyline representations prevent Model from generating ambiguous results, as it consistently encodes direction information. In contrast, Rasterized methods are prone to falsely generating loopy curves (blue ellipses).

figures.description: Grid of multi-view camera images from the dataset
Qualitative comparison of map generation results between Ground Truth, HDMapNet, STSU, and VectorMapNet

sections.title: Centerline Generation
content: To demonstrate the flexibility of polyline, we expand VectorMapNet to predict centerlines.
A benefit of posing map learning as a detection problem: The model can identify pedestrian crossings at intersections that are missed in the annotations of the HD map provided by the dataset.

figures.description: Visualization of centerline prediction across different camera views
Comparison of Ground Truth, HDMapNet, and VectorMapNet for centerline generation
Detailed visualization of centerline generation results compared to Ground Truth
